abstract: Bioactive peptides are integrated sequences within protein structures that remain latent until released through enzymatic hydrolysis, at which point they exert specific physiological functions. Recent research has increasingly focused on characterizing bioactive peptides derived from diverse plant, animal, and microbial sources. These molecules are typically produced via hydrolysis using plant- or microbe-derived enzymes, digestive proteases, or through fermentation with proteolytic starter cultures. The specific amino acid composition and sequence of these peptides dictate their functional versatility, which encompasses antioxidative, antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, cholesterol-lowering, and antihypertensive properties, as well as roles in immune modulation and solute binding. Identification and isolation of these peptides from complex hydrolysates are generally achieved through membrane separation, various chromatographic techniques, and mass spectrometry. Ultimately, the successful integration of bioactive peptides into health-promoting or therapeutic applications hinges on verifying their biostability, bioavailability, and overall safety profiles.
